description: Heading-like Title Case documentation.
Tutorial-style documentation that narrates obvious implementation steps.
Package-qualified calls that do not match locally indexed symbols for the imported package.
Same-package calls to symbols not present in the scanned local package context.
Function names that are overly generic without stronger contextual signals.
Very long identifiers with too many descriptive tokens.
Signatures that rely on any or empty interface types.
Secret-like identifiers assigned direct string literals instead of environment lookups.
Tests that assert success expectations without any obvious negative-path signal.
Tests that look skipped, TODO-shaped, or otherwise placeholder-like.
Tests that exercise production code without an obvious assertion or failure signal.
Operational endpoints registered alongside public routes with no clear boundary.
API-facing error payload structs living in persistence or business packages instead of transport-boundary packages.
Long example payload blocks hardcoded in handlers rather than doc or example helpers.
Shared resources with visible startup paths but no obvious shutdown owner or lifecycle wrapper.
Audit writes that happen before the service has confirmed success.
Repeated auth, tenant, or principal extraction that should live in middleware or a shared boundary helper.
Goroutines launched while relying on open transaction state.
Scheduler or worker registration hidden inside Gin transport packages.
Route or middleware setup that also starts unrelated background jobs.
Endpoints where middleware parses the body and the handler also owns body binding.
Startup code that hides dependency construction inside route registration functions.
Handlers that start querying or writing before finishing request validation.
Cache mutation or eviction that happens before a write transaction commits.
Request parsing or binding errors that are translated into `500` responses instead of client-error status codes.
`main` or `cmd` packages that contain business-rule branches instead of wiring and startup concerns.
Repeated column-name strings outside repository or query packages.
Transaction end-state logic spread across helper functions with unclear ownership.
Constructors that call `os.Getenv` instead of receiving normalized config.
A single DTO reused for create and update when validation rules clearly differ.
Imports where package names clearly violate a layered direction such as `repository -> handler` or `model -> gin`.
Service methods coordinating several repositories with no explicit shared transaction or consistency boundary.
Repeated recover or panic-to-response logic inside handlers instead of centralized recovery middleware.
Per-request validator registration instead of startup-time validator wiring.
Concrete DB arguments erased to `any` or `interface{}` immediately before query execution.
Repeated defaulting logic for pagination, filters, or booleans across handlers.
Domain enums, statuses, and lifecycle constants that live in handler packages instead of domain-facing packages.
Core domain entities that are directly annotated for API serialization without a deliberate boundary exception.
Business error types declared only in handlers instead of a reusable domain or service boundary.
The same entity ID being logged under many different keys across packages.
Handlers that own transport-to-model mapping instead of dedicated mappers or services.
Repeated string error codes without a centralized catalog.
Event or message publication before durable commit is confirmed.
Network calls performed while a DB transaction is open.
Handlers or services that query feature flags directly without a focused flag interface.
Functions that validate upload metadata and also persist the upload in the same boundary step.
Catch-all repositories that depend on reflection-heavy generic CRUD instead of bounded repository APIs.
Generic service names and methods that hide business intent instead of naming the actual use case.
Oversized model structs that appear to merge multiple bounded contexts into one persistence shape.
Code that passes `*gin.Context` into goroutines, repositories, or long-lived service objects instead of extracting `context.Context` and request values.
Package-level `*gin.Engine` singletons reused across unrelated startup paths.
Handlers that try to bind or manually parse multiple body DTOs in one request path.
Handlers that bind request bodies directly into GORM or persistence structs.
Handlers that coordinate several repositories directly, which usually signals missing service orchestration.
Handlers that implement retry loops or backoff decisions inline instead of delegating to service or client layers.
Handlers that execute literal SQL or repository-unaware raw queries.
Handlers that serve fundamentally different transport modes from one function.
Handlers that read feature flags, tenant configuration, or rollout toggles directly instead of using injected policy/config abstractions.
Handlers that manually build several unrelated success payload shapes instead of delegating to clearer endpoints or render helpers.
Handlers that serialize database models directly to the client instead of mapping to response DTOs.
Handlers that contain complex permission matrices instead of delegating to policy or service logic.
Handlers that create or own transactions instead of delegating transactional work to services or unit-of-work helpers.
Code that saves `*gin.Context` onto long-lived structs.
Handlers that branch on `action`, `type`, or mode strings to serve many workflows from one endpoint function.
Handlers that reach for global DB, logger, cache, or config variables instead of injected dependencies.
Repeated route path literals that should be centralized or grouped more intentionally.
Route registration that uses many inline anonymous handlers instead of named handler methods or functions.
Codebases that bootstrap persistence with GORM but run repository queries through raw SQL without a clear adapter boundary.
Handlers that construct `Where`, `Joins`, `Scopes`, or `Order` chains directly.
Services that own detailed ORM query shape instead of delegating to repositories.
Hooks that reach beyond the owning aggregate and write unrelated records.
`FOR UPDATE` or similar locking behavior built in handlers or services instead of repository-owned methods.
GORM models that accumulate transport validation and binding concerns.
Persistence structs that carry API-only derived presentation fields.
Model hooks that depend on higher-layer services or repositories.
Hooks that call HTTP clients, queues, file I/O, or external side effects.
GORM-backed structs with `gorm` tags declared outside `model` or `models` packages unless an explicit `entity` or `schema` package already exists.
Repeated ad hoc scope functions instead of shared named scopes.
Code above the repository layer that changes `Session`, `Clauses`, or transaction options directly.
Duplicated logging ownership where both service and handler log the same failure.
Handler packages that import `database/sql`, `sqlx`, or `pgx` and execute queries directly.
Handler packages that import `gorm.io/gorm` and build query chains directly.
Handlers that bypass a visible service layer and call repositories or query helpers directly for business orchestration.
Handlers that call `Begin`, `Transaction`, or unit-of-work start helpers directly.
Handlers that branch on `err.Error()` text rather than typed errors.
Handler tests that hit real DB setup even though repository or service seams exist.
Health endpoints that depend on full business repositories instead of focused probes.
Generic `helper`, `common`, or `utils` packages that host concrete domain decisions.
`init()` functions that register handlers, routes, or runtime dependencies.
Repeated error-to-status translation switches across handlers.
Entrypoints that do too much without startup composition helpers.
Handlers that re-check required fields manually even though struct-tag validation already exists.
Update maps built from request payloads and passed through layers without typed ownership.
Mapper functions scattered across handlers and services when the repo already has a dedicated mapping package.
Repeated field mapping blocks across multiple handlers.
Handlers that hand-build metric labels repeatedly instead of using focused observation helpers.
Double translation layers where middleware and handlers both map the same error family to responses.
Middleware that reaches into repositories instead of delegating through focused services or auth abstractions.
Middleware that performs domain workflows instead of cross-cutting concerns such as auth, tracing, or request enrichment.
Middleware that writes business entities or persistence models as a side effect of request plumbing.
Middleware that owns transaction lifetime for all downstream business logic.
Middleware that opens database transactions for the whole request path by default.
Shared middleware types and constructors declared in handlers or services instead of a middleware-focused package.
Middleware that depends on mutable globals instead of injected config and logger instances.
Middleware that emits domain success payloads instead of leaving response bodies to handlers or render helpers.
Request handlers that can trigger migration or seed workflows.
One startup path that always runs migrations and serves traffic together.
Migration-specific tests located in transport packages.
Many copy-pasted mock repository structs with the same method sets.
`model` or `models` packages that import transport-facing libraries or handler DTO packages.
Handlers that manually shape every response from persistence structs rather than using dedicated mapping helpers.
One DTO being filled from body, query, and path without a clear precedence policy.
Endpoints in the same API surface that serialize unrelated error payload structures.
Repository or service APIs that encode missing records as `nil, nil` with no explicit result contract.
Response or request DTOs that expose `sql.Null*` types instead of boundary-friendly contract types.
CLI or cron entrypoints that import Gin-only service contracts instead of neutral application services.
Repository APIs that use optional `tx *gorm.DB` parameters instead of explicit unit-of-work contracts.
Raw sort expressions duplicated in handlers and services instead of query helpers.
Mutable package globals used as live config in request paths.
Packages whose names advertise one role, such as `service` or `model`, but whose exported symbols primarily belong to another role.
Repeated pagination DTO binding and defaulting logic across handler files.
API modules that hand-roll page and page-size bounds in many handlers instead of a shared contract.
PATCH-like DTOs that cannot distinguish omitted fields from zero values.
Repeated `strconv` or UUID path-param parsing logic that should live in helpers.
Persistence models that are tied to form or URI binding tags.
Seed entrypoints in production code that are still TODO-shaped or effectively no-ops.
Preload decisions spread across handlers, services, and repositories instead of one query owner.
Handlers that merge several request sources inline instead of normalizing inputs before service calls.
Direct serialization of SQL or GORM error text into API responses.
Literal SQL strings declared and executed from handlers.
Literal SQL strings executed from services instead of repositories or query packages.
README seeding guidance that points to seed code which is still placeholder-like.
README migration guidance that claims explicit migration tooling while startup code still uses `AutoMigrate` without a matching migration path.
Repositories that accept `*gin.Context` rather than `context.Context`.
Repositories that accept transport DTOs instead of repository inputs or domain values.
Repositories that quietly start transactions inside generic CRUD methods.
Repository constructors that take transport objects.
Repository packages that import Gin, `net/http`, or transport-only response helpers.
Repositories that import service packages, which inverts the expected dependency flow.
Repositories that log transport metadata instead of persistence-domain details.
Repositories that expose scope-building fragments instead of complete query APIs.
Repository methods that mix raw SQL and ORM clauses with no clear boundary.
Core business packages that depend on documentation-only tooling or generator-specific concerns.
Repositories that leak `*gorm.DB`, `sqlx.NamedStmt`, query builders, or raw rows to higher layers instead of returning results or domain abstractions.
Repositories that return partially built `*gorm.DB` chains for callers to finish.
Repositories that construct transport-layer error objects or status-code wrappers.
Repositories that know about response contracts instead of returning persistence or domain results.
Single-record-oriented repository updates or deletes that never inspect `RowsAffected` or an equivalent write-outcome signal.
Repository tests that assert HTTP DTOs or handler-layer types.
Repository implementations declared outside `repository`, `repo`, or `store` packages when the repo already uses those roles elsewhere.
Repeated current-user, tenant, locale, or auth extraction logic.
Request DTOs that carry persistence-only `gorm` tags.
Request binding structs that live in repositories or service packages instead of transport-facing packages.
Repeated request-id creation logic spread across handlers.
Inconsistent request-log field sets across the same API module.
Response payload structs that carry persistence-only `gorm` tags.
Response envelope structs that live in persistence or repository packages instead of transport or API contract packages.
Response shapes that silently drop fields that should be stable parts of the API contract.
Repeated success-response envelope formatting that should live in a renderer or transport helper.
Layered service repos that keep the primary binary at repository-root `main.go` instead of `cmd/<name>/main.go`.
Code that constructs or mutates route groups outside the startup router layer.
Repeated manual renaming between route params and DTO fields that lacks a shared mapper.
Router setup files that build queries, mutate models, or execute business actions while registering routes.
Tests that rebuild the full application bootstrap repeatedly instead of using shared test setup.
Route registration split across unrelated files without a clear `router`, `routes`, or `transport` home.
Router setup that creates DB clients, external clients, or repositories directly instead of receiving prebuilt dependencies.
Gin router or handler setup code that also performs migrations.
Duplicated construction of the same service or client in several startup files.
The same error type being converted to different HTTP status codes in the same module.
Service methods that sometimes reuse a transaction and sometimes open one themselves.
A single struct type reused for request binding, database persistence, and API response serialization.
Nested transaction or savepoint control scattered across services and repositories.
Service packages that reach upward into handler helpers for parsing, rendering, or Gin-specific utilities.
Services configured by `map[string]any` or similar untyped blobs.
Constructors that create repositories, clients, or loggers internally instead of accepting them explicitly.
Services that own transport abort behavior.
Service signatures that accept request DTOs declared in handler or transport packages.
Service packages that depend on `github.com/gin-gonic/gin` instead of transport-neutral inputs.
Signatures that mix transport DTOs and GORM models in the same service API.
Service methods that take `*gin.Context` instead of domain inputs plus `context.Context`.
Services that use map-shaped dynamic input instead of typed request contracts.
Services that branch on action strings to implement many create, update, or delete variants in one method.
Services that parse page, sort, filter, or URL values that should already be normalized at the transport boundary.
Services that extract headers, cookies, forms, or route params directly.
Services that depend on transport-specific status constants, abort helpers, or Gin response behavior.
Services that rewrite handler DTOs instead of mapping to service-layer inputs.
Services that leak ORM or driver primitives upward instead of returning business results.
Services that return raw HTTP status codes or transport response metadata.
Services that return dynamic maps instead of typed result contracts.
Services that return `gin.H`, `http.ResponseWriter`, or response-writing helpers.
Services that return final client-facing message strings instead of typed domain errors.
Services that hand transaction objects back to handlers or controllers.
Services with too many unrelated dependencies, which often signals a god-service.
Service-level tests that depend on Gin when the service contract is supposed to be transport-neutral.
Concrete use-case services declared outside `service` or `services` packages in layered web-service repos.
Write-style service methods that mostly forward to repositories without visible domain guard checks.
Code that mutates shared `*gorm.DB` state and reuses it as if it were immutable configuration.
Repeated app, DB, or router bootstrapping that is not moved into shared test support.
Generic packages that mix services, models, DTOs, validators, and queries behind one broad shared namespace.
Repeated `deleted_at IS NULL` style filters rather than a shared repository or scope policy.
Repeated client-field whitelist logic across endpoints.
Repository methods that leak driver-specific null wrappers beyond the persistence boundary.
Raw query templates stored in handlers, services, or DTO packages instead of repository-adjacent files.
SQL-shape assertions written at handler level instead of repository tests.
Code outside repositories that manually scans rows into structs.
Endpoints that blend success and error shapes into one ambiguous contract.
Persistence structs used as the public docs contract instead of transport DTOs.
Oversized case tables that mix unrelated behaviors into one monolithic test.
Repeated table-name strings across handlers, services, and jobs.
Repeated table-name or model-scope customization across many model files without a shared convention.
Production startup code that imports test-only bootstrap helpers.
Fixture and factory helpers defined in non-test production files.
Near-identical helper builders or setup code duplicated across test packages.
Brittle raw-JSON string assertions when typed response DTOs exist.
API tests that assert persistence model shapes instead of response contracts.
Handler tests that mock Gin internals directly instead of using `httptest` when boundary behavior matters.
Production no-op or dummy types that appear to exist mainly to satisfy tooling instead of runtime behavior.
Repeated raw span-name literals across handlers and services.
Both repository and handler layers translating the same transaction errors.
Transaction helpers buried in handlers or services instead of a repository or unit-of-work package.
Transaction handles passed through multiple layers as ordinary arguments.
Response code strings that have no shared type or catalog package.
Repositories that record HTTP-route or handler-oriented metrics.
Transport tests that skip the service seam and assert repository behavior.
`Unscoped()` usage in generic repository methods without a clearly dangerous or admin-only name.
PATCH-style repository writes that rely on struct zero-value semantics without explicit field intent.
Provider packages that declare an interface even though upstream packages appear to own the abstraction seam.
Handlers in the same API module that emit different validation error contracts.
Repeated required-field or enum-validation blocks across multiple handler files.
Reusable validator code that reaches into repositories instead of receiving precomputed facts or focused services.
Reusable validators that are scattered across handlers instead of living in a dedicated validation helper package.
Repeated filter templates that should be shared through scopes or query builders.
Potentially blocking calls observed between Lock and Unlock.
Likely long-lived goroutines launched after a derived context is created and before the matching cancel call is observed.
Raw go statements launched from inside loops without obvious WaitGroup coordination.
Raw go statements without an obvious context or WaitGroup-like coordination signal.
Looping goroutine literals without an obvious ctx.Done() or done-channel shutdown path.
Repeated Lock or RLock acquisition inside loops.
sync.RWMutex usage without a clear read-heavy access pattern.
WaitGroup-based goroutine fan-out that still carries explicit error-path coordination better suited to errgroup.
Struct field tags that repeat the same key more than once.
Struct field tags that do not parse as valid Go tag key/value pairs.
Methods on the same receiver type mix pointer and value receivers.
select { default: ... } inside loops, which often spins instead of blocking.
Cache-oriented interfaces whose IO-style methods omit context.Context.
Cache implementations that call context.Background() or context.TODO() instead of propagating caller context.
Functions or request handlers that already have a caller-owned context available but still create context.Background() or context.TODO() locally.
context.WithValue keys or exported key declarations that use built-in scalar types instead of unexported key types.
Functions that accept context.Context but not as the first non-receiver parameter on context-aware call paths.
Struct fields that store context.Context instead of keeping context request-scoped through method parameters.
context.WithValue used for dependency-like objects or payload-like data instead of lightweight request metadata.
Derived contexts where deslop cannot find a local cancel() or defer cancel() call.
Standard-library context-aware calls from functions that do not accept context.Context.
Functions that already accept context.Context but still call context-free stdlib APIs like http.Get or exec.Command.
Background goroutines that still appear to inherit request-scoped context instead of detaching with context.WithoutCancel or equivalent.
time.Sleep inside loops — often indicates polling or busy-wait style code.
GORM Association(...).Find(...) loaders observed inside loops.
AutoMigrate or schema probes running on request paths instead of startup.
Bun DB handles created directly on request paths instead of reused process-level state.
Bun select-and-scan request paths without a visible limit or pagination marker.
DB pool sizing or lifetime settings changed on request paths.
COUNT(...) or GORM Count(...) observed inside loops.
Request-path GORM flows that run Count(...) and then a broad Find(...) with the same filter shape.
GORM .Create(...) used inside loops with no visible CreateInBatches(...) path in the same function.
DATE/CAST wrapping indexed columns in WHERE clauses, preventing index usage.
database Ping(...) or PingContext(...) called on request paths instead of startup or explicit health checks.
Bulk creates performed with GORM default transaction enabled — SkipDefaultTransaction improves throughput.
GORM Delete(...) chains observed inside loops one row at a time.
Distinct on wide rows without Select projection — a key-only subquery is usually cheaper.
Initial Find followed by a separate Preload query that could be folded into one.
ent clients opened directly inside request handlers instead of shared startup wiring.
Exec(...) or ExecContext(...) used for row-by-row SQL writes inside loops.
COUNT(*) used for an existence check instead of EXISTS or LIMIT 1.
All rows fetched and then sliced in Go instead of using database-level pagination.
Unbounded result sets that could use FindInBatches or cursor iteration.
GORM FirstOrCreate(...) chains observed inside loops.
GORM debug logging enabled on request paths.
Request-path GORM Find(...) chains with no visible Limit(...) step.
GORM chains combining Joins, Preload, and unbounded Find on request paths.
gorm.Open(...) called on request paths instead of process-level setup.
Request-path GORM chains that use Preload(clause.Associations) or other broad preload graphs.
GORM queries on wide models without a Select projection to limit fetched columns.
GORM Session(...) chains allocated inside loops before issuing queries.
LIKE queries with leading wildcards that prevent index usage.
LOWER() or other functions wrapping indexed columns in WHERE clauses, preventing index usage.
Query chains with many OR filter conditions that often scale poorly.
Multiple transactions started on a single request path.
Request-path GORM Find(...) chains that page with Offset(...), which often scales poorly on large lists.
ORDER BY RAND()/RANDOM() observed on request paths.
ORM chains that order results without a Limit on request paths.
pgx.CollectRows used on request paths without a visible LIMIT in the query.
Repeated pgxpool acquire calls inside loops that may churn pooled resources.
pgxpool pools created on request paths instead of reused application-level state.
Per-request pgxpool connectivity probes that add latency outside health-check boundaries.
GORM Preload(...) queries configured and executed inside loops.
Prepare(...) or PrepareContext(...) observed inside loops.
The same literal SQL is prepared multiple times on one request path.
QueryRow(...) or QueryRowContext(...) used inside loops for point lookups that usually want a bulk prefetch path.
GORM Raw(...).Scan(...) chains observed inside loops.
Redis clients created per request instead of reused as shared process infrastructure.
Redis command loops that issue round-trips without pipeline or batch usage.
Per-request Redis ping checks instead of startup or explicit health-probe validation.
The same query template executed multiple times in one function.
Upsert-style writes executed row by row inside loops instead of batched.
New struct allocated for each row scan instead of reusing a scratch variable.
GORM Save used for a single-column update instead of a targeted Update call.
GORM Save(...) writes full models inside loops.
Rows scanned into map[string]any instead of typed structs on hot paths.
ORM lookups (Select, Get, First, etc.) executed inside loops.
database/sql pools opened on request paths instead of process-level setup.
sqlx.Select used on request paths without a visible LIMIT in the query.
Transactions started inside loops instead of once around the wider batch.
IN clauses built from request-driven collections without bound limits.
GORM Update(...), UpdateColumn(...), or Updates(...) calls observed inside loops one row at a time.
GORM Updates(map[string]...) maps allocated inside loops for per-row updates.
Blank identifier assignments that discard an err-like value.
fmt.Errorf calls that reference err without %w.
err != nil branches that jump straight to panic or log.Fatal style exits.
Gin handlers that bind JSON into map[string]any or map[string]interface{} on hot request paths.
Gin handlers that bind query parameters into map[string]any or map[string]interface{} on hot request paths.
Request-path handlers that dump full HTTP requests or responses with httputil.
Same upstream URL called multiple times in one handler.
Environment variable reads observed on request paths instead of cached configuration.
errgroup goroutine fanout without a visible concurrency limit in handlers.
Request-path handlers that read files directly instead of using startup caching or dedicated file-serving paths.
Gin handlers that open uploaded form files and then materialize them with io.ReadAll(...).
Gin handlers that read GetRawData() and later bind JSON from the same request body.
Gin handlers that call c.Copy() once per loop iteration before goroutine fanout.
Verbose body or payload logging observed on likely high-volume Gin routes.
Gzip or zip writers recreated per chunk inside handler loops instead of reusing per stream.
IndentedJSON(...) used on a request path instead of compact JSON rendering.
Handlers that manually marshal JSON and then write it through gin.Context.Data(...).
Export data written in loops without visible buffering in handlers.
Large gin.H payloads built as transient dynamic maps right before JSON rendering.
LoadHTMLGlob or LoadHTMLFiles called on request paths instead of startup initialization.
Database or GORM connections opened inside handlers or middleware instead of process-level setup.
HTTP client allocated inside Gin handlers or middleware instead of being shared.
Regexp compilation inside Gin handlers instead of using precompiled patterns.
Middleware or helper chains that parse the request body after the main handler has already bound it.
Gin handlers that bind the request body multiple times in one function.
Request handlers that drive row-by-row DB writes with no batch path.
Large list or export handlers that materialize everything before writing rather than using chunked or streaming output.
Gin handlers that call ParseMultipartForm(...) with large in-memory thresholds on request paths.
Gin handlers that materialize c.Request.Body with io.ReadAll(...) and then bind the same body again.
Gin handlers that read, rewind, and decode the same request body multiple times.
Gin handlers that call c.JSON(...) or c.PureJSON(...) from inside loops.
Large map-literal response assembly on hot routes where a stable typed response would be cheaper.
Handlers that load files into memory and then write them through gin.Context.Data(...) instead of using file helpers or streaming.
Gin handlers that use ShouldBindBodyWith(...) even though only one body bind is observed.
Template construction or parsing inside Gin handlers instead of startup-time caching.
Upstream HTTP calls made per item inside handler loops.
One upstream HTTP response body decoded into multiple targets in the same handler.
Slice sorting observed inside loops — often cheaper to sort once after the loop.
Slice append followed by reslice each iteration instead of batching.
File or socket reads inside loops without visible bufio buffering.
File or socket writes inside loops without visible bufio buffering.
strings.Builder, bytes.Buffer, or bytes.NewBuffer(...) constructions observed inside loops instead of being reset or reused.
Byte-to-string or string-to-byte conversion observed inside loops in short-lived lookup or append paths.
bytes.Buffer used without Grow when approximate output size is locally visible.
The same byte-slice input is passed through bytes.Split* or bytes.Fields* helpers multiple times in one function.
csv.Writer.Flush() called inside per-row loops, reducing buffering effectiveness.
Same collection traversed multiple times for filter, count, and process steps.
gzip.NewReader(...) or gzip.NewWriter(...) recreated inside iterative paths instead of per stream.
json.NewDecoder(...) constructed repeatedly inside loops instead of reusing a stable decoder per stream.
json.NewEncoder(...) constructed repeatedly inside loops instead of reusing a stable encoder per stream.
make(map[K]V, ...) scratch maps recreated inside loops instead of being reused or prebuilt.
make([]T, ...) scratch slices recreated inside loops instead of being reused.
Map insertions inside loops without a visible size hint on the initial make call.
Append calls inside nested loops without visible preallocation on the outer slice.
Nested-loop lookups or joins that could use a map index for O(1) access.
io.ReadAll(...) materializes a payload and the same binding is then unmarshaled again instead of using a streaming decode path.
regexp.Compile or regexp.MustCompile observed inside obvious iterative paths.
maps.Clone or equivalent map-copy calls observed inside loops.
slices.Clone(...) or similar whole-slice cloning observed inside loops.
Slice append inside a range loop without visible preallocation when the bound is locally known.
slices.Contains(...) or slices.Index(...) used inside loops against a stable-looking slice binding.
Sorting a collection when only the first element or min/max is needed.
Stable value normalization (ToLower, TrimSpace, etc.) repeated inside inner loops.
The same string binding is converted with strconv parsing helpers multiple times in one function.
strings.Builder used without Grow when approximate output size is locally visible.
The same string input is passed through strings.Split* or strings.Fields* helpers multiple times in one function.
html/template or text/template parse calls observed on request-style paths instead of startup-time caching.
time.Parse(...) or ParseInLocation(...) observed inside loops with a stable layout.
url.Parse(...) or ParseRequestURI(...) observed inside loops with a stable-looking base input.
UUID or hash formatting observed inside loops only for log output.
Repository automation that does not visibly run `go test -race` for Go code.
Bootstrap code that opens long-lived DB clients without visible pool sizing or lifetime limits.
defer statements inside loops that can accumulate resources until function exit.
The same locally created channel appears to be closed more than once in one function body.
File handles opened via os.Open, os.Create, or os.OpenFile without an observed Close() path.
http.Client literals allocated on regular call paths instead of being reused as shared client state.
Local http.Client{} literals constructed without an explicit timeout.
HTTP responses acquired locally without an observed resp.Body.Close() call.
HTTP response bodies that are closed without being drained or otherwise consumed when the response is ignored.
HTTP server startup paths that lack visible signal handling and Shutdown ownership for graceful shutdown.
Explicit http.Server{} values that omit common timeout fields.
Response decoding or body consumption that happens without an observed StatusCode check.
Handlers that write the response body before calling WriteHeader(...).
init() functions that perform network, file-system, or subprocess side effects.
Package-level variables that are mutated from function bodies instead of kept immutable.
Wrapper structs that mostly forward one-to-one through an interface field with little added policy.
Exported functions or methods that expose raw boolean mode switches in their signatures.
Functions that range over a locally owned channel without an observed close path.
HTTP request bodies read or decoded without an observed size limit such as io.LimitReader or http.MaxBytesReader.
Rows iterated with Next() but without a visible final Rows.Err() check.
Query result handles that appear locally owned but have no observed rows.Close() call.
A locally owned channel is closed and later used in a send expression.
Repository-local interfaces with one obvious implementation and a very small consumer surface.
Loop-heavy or slow work performed while a transaction appears open.
Prepared statements or similar DB handles without an observed Close() call.
time.NewTicker(...) is created without an observed Stop() call.
time.After(...) is allocated inside loops instead of reusing a timer or deadline.
net/http helper calls or http.DefaultClient usage that carry no explicit application timeout.
Transactions begun and later committed with no observed rollback guard.
`credentials.NewStaticCredentials("AKID...", "secret...", "")` with literal access keys
`session.NewSession()` or `config.LoadDefaultConfig(ctx)` inside handler functions
`cmd.Flags().GetString("flag")` inside `RunE` when the flag could be bound to a variable with `StringVar`
`os.ReadFile("config.json")` or `viper.ReadInConfig()` inside handler or loop code
`dynamodb.Scan` in request handler functions
multiple `os.Getenv` + `strconv.Atoi` / `strconv.ParseBool` chains that could be replaced with a config struct + `envconfig` or `env` library
`log.Error(err); return err` or `logger.Error("failed", zap.Error(err)); return fmt.Errorf("failed: %w", err)` — logging the error then returning it
gRPC handler methods that create new `context.Background()` instead of using the stream/request context
`grpc.Dial(addr, opts...)` or `grpc.NewClient(addr, opts...)` inside handler functions
unary RPC handlers returning or receiving messages > 4MB (inferred from large slice/struct serialization patterns)
`grpc.NewServer()` without keepalive server parameters in long-running services
interceptor/middleware construction inside RPC handler methods instead of at server setup

JWT verification code that accepts `"none"` or `alg: ""` as valid signing methods, or uses `jwt.Parse` without `WithValidMethods`

`exec.Command(userInput)` or `exec.Command("sh", "-c", variable)` where the command string appears to come from a function parameter or request binding
`recover()` in HTTP middleware that sends the panic message/stack to the response writer
struct fields named `Password`, `Passwd`, or `Pwd` stored as `string` in database model structs without evidence of hashing
map reads/writes from multiple goroutines without mutex or `sync.Map` protection (detect goroutine launches + shared map access patterns)

`log.Printf`, `slog.Info`, `zap.String`, `logrus.WithField` calls that include variables named `password`, `secret`, `token`, `apiKey`, `creditCard`, `ssn`, or similar
goroutines appending to a shared slice without synchronization
`smtp.PlainAuth` used without TLS (`smtp.SendMail` to non-TLS endpoints)
Query execution calls where SQL is constructed dynamically with concatenation or fmt.Sprintf.

`http.Get(userInput)` or `http.NewRequest("GET", userInput, nil)` where the URL comes from request parameters
exported struct fields containing sensitive data (Password, Secret, Token, APIKey, PrivateKey) without `json:"-"` tags in API response structs
`os.Create("/tmp/myapp-data.txt")` or `os.OpenFile("/tmp/" + fixedName, ...)` with predictable filenames
`template.HTML(userInput)` or `template.JS(userInput)` type conversions on data from request parameters
`text/template` used to generate HTML content (check for HTML tags in template literals or `.html` file extensions in `ParseFiles`)
`token == expectedToken` or `bytes.Equal(token, expected)` for comparing authentication tokens, API keys, or HMAC values

`md5.New()`, `sha1.New()`, `md5.Sum()`, `sha1.Sum()` used for integrity checks, checksums, or MAC operations (not just `weak_crypto` import-level detection)
`websocket.Upgrader{CheckOrigin: func(r *http.Request) bool { return true }}` or missing `CheckOrigin`
`os.OpenFile(path, flag, 0666)` or `os.WriteFile(path, data, 0777)` with world-readable/writable permissions

`yaml.Unmarshal(untrustedInput, &target)` using `gopkg.in/yaml.v2` without size limits
Directories that mix base Go package names after ignoring the _test suffix.
Import blocks that place stdlib imports after third-party imports.
Unusually enthusiastic or emoji-heavy production comments.
File mixes snake_case and camelCase function naming conventions.
Comments that narrate obvious implementation steps instead of explaining intent.
Very small helper functions that have unusually long, textbook-style docstrings.
Heavy ecosystem imports with little local evidence of real need.
Highly similar non-test function bodies repeated across multiple Python files.
Project repeats the same long string literal across multiple files.
Repeated exception-handling block shapes in one file.
Repository repeats the same SQL-like query fragment across multiple files.
Highly similar utility logic shared between test and production code.
Repository repeats the same data transformation pipeline stages across multiple functions.
Repeated validation guard pipelines across functions in one file.
Project repeats the same long string literal multiple times in one file.
Celery tasks dispatch .delay(...) or .apply_async(...) inside loops without an obvious canvas primitive such as group() or chord().
Celery tasks synchronously wait on AsyncResult.get(...) instead of handing work off asynchronously.
Celery task bodies repeatedly read environment configuration instead of using startup-time bootstrap.
click or typer commands parse config files on each invocation instead of using shared bootstrap or dependency setup.
click or typer commands scatter repeated environment lookups through the command body.
click or typer commands allocate HTTP clients inside command bodies instead of reusing a shared client factory.
Django views call .all() without visible pagination, slicing, or limiting.
Django code creates one model at a time inside loops instead of using bulk_create().
Django code deletes individual models inside loops instead of using set-based queryset deletion.
Django views or request paths reference schema or migration operations that belong in migrations.
Django queryset iteration shows N+1 risk with no visible select_related(...) or prefetch_related(...).
Django querysets use count() for existence checks instead of exists().
The same Django queryset appears to be evaluated multiple times in one function.
len(queryset) is used where queryset.count() would avoid loading every row.
Django querysets use order_by(\"?\") or equivalent random ordering on request paths.
Django request or service code executes raw SQL inside loops instead of batching.
Django code saves full model instances in loops without update_fields or bulk updates.
Django code updates one row at a time inside loops instead of using bulk or set-based updates.
Django loops hydrate full model instances where values(), values_list(), or only() would likely be cheaper.
FastAPI background task dispatches appear to rely on default exception behavior without visible error handling.
FastAPI dependencies construct HTTP clients per request instead of using app lifespan or shared setup.
FastAPI response models use ORM conversion paths without visible ORM compatibility configuration.
FastAPI sync route handlers perform blocking I/O instead of using async routes or executor offload.
Flask views repeatedly read app.config on request paths instead of consuming bootstrapped settings.
Flask code enables debug=True in application runtime paths.
Flask views read files directly on request paths instead of using cached or static responses.
Flask views create database connections per request instead of using pooled or app-scoped access.
Flask views instantiate JSON encoders per request instead of reusing app-level serialization setup.
Flask views build large in-memory responses where generator or streaming responses would fit better.
Flask request handlers parse the same request body multiple times.
Flask views render templates from inline strings instead of using file-backed templates.
Handlers build large inline dict responses where typed response models would be clearer and cheaper.
Middleware compiles regex patterns per request instead of precompiling them.
Middleware creates HTTP clients per request instead of reusing app-scoped clients.
Middleware loads config files on request paths instead of using startup configuration.
Pydantic v2 code serializes model_dump() output through json.dumps(...) instead of using model_dump_json().
Pydantic v2 validation is preceded by json.loads(...) even though model_validate_json() could validate raw JSON directly.
Handlers manually json.dumps(...) payloads and then wrap them in framework Response objects.
SQLAlchemy sessions commit inside loops instead of batching changes and committing once.
SQLAlchemy engines are created on request or handler paths instead of being process-scoped.
Async SQLAlchemy sessions rely on the default expire_on_commit behavior instead of making the async access pattern explicit.
SQLAlchemy query shapes suggest lazy-loaded N+1 access with no visible eager loading.
SQLAlchemy code issues queries inside loops instead of batching or prefetching.
SQLAlchemy Session objects are created without context-manager or close handling.
SQLModel sessions commit inside loops instead of applying one transaction after batched updates.
SQLModel Session.exec(...) is called inside loops instead of combining the query shape.
Handlers execute SQLModel select().all() paths without visible limits or pagination.
Template rendering appears inside loops instead of rendering once over prepared data.
Request handlers issue upstream HTTP calls without visible timeout configuration.
Request handlers make sequential upstream HTTP calls inside loops instead of batching or bounded concurrency.
Handlers decode upstream responses without visible status checks such as raise_for_status() or status_code guards.
A collection is appended to and then sorted on each iteration instead of sorting once after accumulation.
csv.Writer flushes on each row instead of buffering a larger batch.
dict.items(), keys(), or values() are repeatedly materialized inside loops.
enumerate(range(len(...))) style loops that add indexing ceremony without extra value.
The same collection is traversed repeatedly for filtering, counting, and later iteration.
Membership tests against list literals where a tuple or set would be clearer or cheaper.
A JSON encoder object is recreated per item instead of being reused for the stream.
The same JSON payload is decoded multiple times inside one function instead of caching the parsed value.
A list comprehension is built only so len(...) can be called on it.
File contents are fully read and then splitlines() is called instead of streaming lines.
readlines() materializes the whole file before line-by-line iteration.
re.compile(...) or similar regex compilation repeated inside hot code paths.
json.dumps(...) is repeated for the same object instead of caching the serialized value.
The same file appears to be opened multiple times within one function.
A sequence is fully sorted even though only the first or smallest element is used.
Repeated startswith(...) or endswith(...) checks that can often be combined into tuple-based calls.
Repeated writes inside loops with no visible buffering or batching.
String or collection concatenation happens inside a comprehension body, creating avoidable churn.
A dict-like source is copied on each loop iteration instead of being reused or hoisted.
gzip open/create calls are repeated per chunk instead of per stream.
A list is copied on each loop iteration even though the source appears unchanged.
Nested linear list searches that look like they want a temporary map or set index.
Path resolution helpers such as resolve() or expanduser() run inside loops on invariant inputs.
pickle.dumps(...) is called repeatedly for the same structural shape in a loop.
datetime.strptime(...) is repeated with the same format string instead of reusing a parsed shape or preprocessing once.
The same dictionary key is fetched repeatedly instead of storing the value in a local binding.
The same hashing algorithm is repeatedly constructed in a loop or tight path.
The same object goes through repeated isinstance(...) checks that could be consolidated.
The same list index lookup is performed repeatedly instead of caching the accessed value.
Locale or codec lookups repeat inside loops instead of being cached once.
An invariant string template is formatted repeatedly in a loop instead of being partially precomputed.
A set with the same elements is rebuilt on each iteration instead of being hoisted.
A collection is sorted even though only the first element or a membership-style check is needed.
String joins that materialize an unnecessary list comprehension instead of using a generator or direct iterable.
Tuple unpacking is repeated in tight loops where reducing per-iteration overhead may help.
urlparse() or urlsplit() is repeated inside loops for invariant base values.
The same XML payload is parsed repeatedly within one function.
The same YAML payload is parsed repeatedly within one function.
Broad except Exception: style handlers that still obscure failure shape even when not fully swallowed.
Loop shapes that look like obvious sum, any, or all candidates.
Blocks of commented-out source code left in production files.
Environment-variable lookups that omit a default value or explicit failure handler.
Direct eval() or exec() usage in non-test Python code.
Broad exception handlers like except: or except Exception: that immediately suppress the error with pass, continue, break, or return.
Request or CLI entry points that trust external input without obvious validation or guard checks.
Hardcoded threshold, rate-limit, or pricing-style literals assigned inside non-test Python functions.
Hardcoded filesystem path literals assigned inside non-test Python functions.
Repeated branch-shaping numeric or string literals that likely want an explicit constant or policy name.
Resource management (files, network connections) inside non-test Python functions that omits with-statement context managers.
Modules that expose public sync and async entry points together.
Request, sync, or job-style Python functions that call HTTP boundaries with no obvious timeout or retry policy.
== None or != None checks instead of is None or is not None.
print() calls left in non-test Python functions that do not look like obvious main-entrypoint output.
Public Python functions that omit complete parameter or return annotations.
Explicit return None in simple code paths where Python would already return None implicitly.
Obvious locally implemented utility helpers that overlap with already-imported standard-library style helpers.
List, set, or dicit comprehensions used as standalone statements where the result is discarded.
Public Python functions that expose *args or **kwargs instead of a clearer interface.
Pipeline-style functions with no visible error handling or recovery path.
Manual dataset batching loops that bypass torch.utils.data.DataLoader.
Embeddings recomputed on request paths instead of cached or precomputed for stable inputs.
Embeddings are compared without visible dimension validation before similarity math.
Whole DataFrames are copied for transforms that could target a smaller subset or reuse views.
Pipeline-style functions mutate global state, making concurrency and reproducibility brittle.
GPU-backed experiment flows show no visible memory or session cleanup between runs.
Hardcoded model-provider API keys or secret-like tokens appear in source.
Multiple intermediate DataFrames accumulate with no visible cleanup in one pipeline.
LangChain or LlamaIndex prompt and chain wiring rebuilt on each request path.
LLM API calls are made inside loops without batching or aggregation.
Large LLM responses are loaded fully into memory instead of streamed or incrementally consumed.
Repeated LLM calls show no visible caching even when prompt inputs appear likely to repeat.
Torch-style inference paths run model(...) without obvious eval() or inference mode setup.
Models are loaded on request paths instead of once during application startup.
Models or tensors are moved to a device repeatedly inside loops.
External JSON or tabular data is parsed without visible schema validation.
np.append(...) is used inside loops, forcing repeated reallocations.
Arrays are constructed and immediately cast, implying a missing upfront dtype choice.
Python loops iterate directly over arrays where vectorized NumPy operations would be clearer and faster.
NumPy arrays are converted to Python lists in hot paths, increasing object overhead.
Arrays are repeatedly stacked inside loops instead of collected and stacked once.
Simple DataFrame transforms are routed through apply(lambda) instead of vectorized operations.
Chained DataFrame assignment patterns risk SettingWithCopy-style behavior.
DataFrames are concatenated inside loops instead of collected and concatenated once.
DataFrames are copied inside loops, amplifying memory churn.
Dynamic string building is fed into pandas eval/query calls, increasing injection and correctness risk.
Full DataFrames are printed or displayed in production-oriented code paths.
DataFrame-transform methods are called without reassignment or inplace=True, silently discarding results.
iterrows() is used on DataFrames instead of vectorized operations or itertuples().
DataFrame merges omit validate= safeguards against multiplicative joins.
pd.read_csv(...) calls omit dtype hints, forcing extra inference work.
Data-loading functions read large tabular files without chunksize or nrows limits.
DataFrame to_dict conversions are repeated inside loops.
Training or evaluation code prints metrics directly instead of using logging or experiment tracking.
Prompt strings are built incrementally inside loops instead of composing a stable template once.
Training or evaluation entrypoints use randomness without an obvious seed.
Rate-limit retries appear without visible backoff or Retry-After handling.
LLM requests are sent without visible token counting or context-window checks.
Tokenizer encode calls repeated inside loops without caching or batching signals.
Tokenizers are loaded on request paths instead of once during application startup.
Torch inference paths show no visible no_grad() or inference_mode() guard.
optimizer.step() appears without an obvious zero_grad() reset.
Vector-store clients created on request paths instead of reused application state.
wandb or mlflow metrics are logged in inner loops instead of batched or reported at coarser boundaries.
Local Python packages reaching into another package's internal or private modules.
pyproject metadata missing an explicit Python runtime requirement.
pyproject script entrypoints that do not resolve to a locally indexed module callable.
Public Python APIs that expose Any in parameter or return contracts.
Synchronous network, subprocess, sleep, or file I/O calls made from async def functions.
Queue-style list operations like pop(0) or insert(0, ...) that may want collections.deque.
Calls that load an entire payload into memory instead of streaming.
list(...)[0] style access that materializes a whole list just to read the first element.
Repeated membership checks against obviously list-like containers inside loops.
Direct recursion in traversal-style helpers that may be safer as iterative walks for deep inputs.
Repeated len(...) checks inside loops when the receiver appears unchanged locally.
Repeated string concatenation inside loops can create O(n^2) growth and extra allocations.
Loop-local list, dict, or set construction that likely adds avoidable allocation churn.
Async lock scopes or explicit acquire/release regions that continue across unrelated await points.
Retry-style async loops that sleep a fixed interval without visible backoff, jitter, or bounded retry policy.
Background task bindings with no obvious await, callback, supervisor, or observation path.
Dataclass __post_init__ methods that perform I/O, subprocess, network, or heavyweight client setup.
Dataclass fields that use mutable defaults instead of default_factory.
Module-scope configuration or secret loading that runs during import instead of an explicit startup path.
Module-scope file reads, writes, or directory scans that happen during import.
Module-scope HTTP or socket calls executed while the module is imported.
Subprocess launches triggered from module scope during import.
Eagerly constructed network, database, or cloud clients bound at module scope.
Function parameters that use mutable defaults such as [], {}, or set() directly in the signature.
Mutable module globals updated from multiple functions.
Dataclass or TypedDict models that accumulate many optional fields and boolean switches.
pickle.load(s) or dill.load(s) style deserialization in production code.
Public functions or model fields that expose Any, object, or similarly wide contracts.
Subprocess boundaries that enable shell=True.
tarfile.extractall(...) without an obvious filter, members list, or path-validation helper.
Temporary files or directories created without a visible cleanup or context-manager ownership path.
Direct indexing of optional TypedDict keys without an obvious guard path.
yaml.load(...) or full_load(...) style loaders used where safe loading is more appropriate.
asyncio.create_task(...) or similar task creation whose handle is discarded immediately.
Repository-local Python class chains with unusually deep inheritance depth.
Constructors that instantiate several collaborators eagerly inside __init__.
Python classes that concentrate unusually high method count, public surface area, and mutable instance state.
Very large Python functions with high control-flow and call-surface concentration.
Functions that mix HTTP, persistence, and filesystem-style concerns in one body.
__init__.py files that carry enough imports and behavior to look like monolithic modules.
Non-__init__.py modules that are unusually large and combine many imports with orchestration-heavy behavior.
Read-style, transformation-style, or utility-style names that still perform mutation or own multiple infrastructure concerns.
Ceremonial wrapper-style or tiny data-container classes that add little beyond storing constructor state.
Modules that depend on a large number of repository-local Python modules.
Classes that assign an unusually large number of instance attributes across their methods.
Arc<Mutex<Option<T>>>-style state bags that hide lifecycle state behind nested mutation layers.
State structs encoded with multiple booleans instead of a dedicated enum.
Public signatures that borrow &PathBuf instead of &Path.
Public signatures that borrow &String instead of &str.
Public signatures that borrow &Vec<T> instead of &[T].
Builders that expose build() without an obvious validation step.
Constructor-like APIs that use multiple boolean flags.
Static or lazy global state wrapped in mutable lock-based containers.
Collection-plus-lock fields embedded directly in public or central state structs.
Config-like structs with many Option fields and no obvious validation path.
Constructor-like functions that return or store partially initialized structs.
Public structs that expose Mutex, RwLock, RefCell, Cell, or similar fields directly.
Public library-facing APIs that return anyhow-style Result types instead of a clearer domain error.
Public APIs that expose a raw boolean mode switch.
Public APIs that expose Box<dyn Error> rather than a clearer error surface.
Domain-style structs built around Rc<RefCell<T>> instead of clearer ownership boundaries.
Required-looking contract fields that opt into #[serde(default)].
#[serde(flatten)] catch-all maps or loose value bags that absorb unknown fields.
Strict-looking config or request structs that deserialize without deny_unknown_fields.
Boundary-facing enums that derive #[serde(untagged)] and risk ambiguous wire formats.
Enum-like boundary fields kept as String instead of a dedicated enum.
A Drop implementation does blocking work that can surface in async contexts.
A permit or pooled resource may be held across an .await.
Related state mutations appear split around an await boundary.
Conflicting lock acquisition order suggests a lock-order cycle.
select! reuse lacks pinning or fusing markers for repeated polling.
An async function may monopolize the executor with blocking work and no await.
A select! loop may recreate futures instead of reusing long-lived ones.
Async work is spawned without an obvious cancellation path.
std::sync::Mutex appears to be held across .await in async code.
Filesystem code that checks metadata or existence before opening a path.
Internal library functions that return anyhow-style error surfaces instead of crate-local errors.
Manual temp-directory setup and cleanup that should usually use RAII helpers.
Numeric narrowing casts that may silently truncate or change precision.
Direct equality or inequality comparisons on secret-like values.
Production code that reads an entire file into a string without a size bound.
Debug is derived on a type that carries secret-like fields.
Default is derived or implemented on a type that likely cannot have a safe default state.
Floating-point storage is used for money-like values.
A boolean toggle is mixed with optional credentials, creating invalid-state combinations.
A defaultable type includes optional secret-like fields, which can hide invalid configuration.
Business-facing data is stored as a raw primitive instead of a stronger domain type.
Deserialize is derived for sensitive fields without obvious validation.
Serialize is derived for secret-like fields that may need redaction or exclusion.
dbg!() left in non-test Rust code.
expect() used in non-test Rust code.
Rust doc comments that still contain a FIXME marker in non-test code.
Rust doc comments that still contain a HACK marker in non-test code.
panic macro left in non-test Rust code.
Rust doc comments that still contain a TODO marker in non-test code.
todo!() left in non-test Rust code.
unimplemented!() left in non-test Rust code.
unreachable!() left in non-test Rust code.
unsafe fn or unsafe block without a nearby SAFETY: comment within the previous two lines.
unwrap() used in non-test Rust code.
Broad dead_code suppression that can hide real wiring or maintenance gaps.
Repeated startup or bootstrap wiring in multiple functions within the same file.
mod.rs files that look like catch-all subsystem dumps.
Rust module files that grow too large and mix too many responsibilities.
Public glob re-exports that flatten the crate surface.
Same-directory #[path = "..."] module attributes that standard resolution could replace.
Crate roots that expose too many public re-exports at once.
Repeated struct-field dereferences inside a loop that may indicate an array-of-structs hot path.
A Drop implementation performs blocking work.
Blocking I/O or blocking work observed in async Rust code.
HashMap default-hasher construction in a likely hot path.
Large allocations may be captured across await points and bloat future size.
.lines() iteration used in a loop where per-item allocation may matter.
A lock appears to be held across an .await boundary.
Path::join used with an absolute segment that discards the existing base path.
Pointer-heavy boxed vector-style storage that may hurt cache locality.
tokio::sync::Mutex used in a fully synchronous critical path with no await.
File-like writes performed inside a loop without buffering or batching.
UTF-8 validation appears in a likely hot path and may deserve profiling.
Axum routers assembled inside handler call paths instead of startup wiring.
Likely heavy state cloned repeatedly inside loops in Rust application code.
Rust request handlers reading environment configuration on the hot request path.
Tokio runtimes created per call instead of being owned at process or bootstrap boundaries.
tonic transport channels dialed on request paths instead of reusing configured clients.
Workspace Cargo manifests with multiple members but no explicit resolver version.
Blocking runtime bridges such as block_in_place or block_on inside request-handling code.
Per-request channel and notification state creation instead of startup-owned coordination.
Spawned background work whose JoinHandle is immediately discarded or never supervised.
Notify/wait coordination that lacks any visible shutdown or cancellation branch.
Process-global environment mutation used as runtime control flow.
Repeated runtime or executor builder setup inside loops or retry bodies.
Unchecked UTF-8 conversion at a repository or service boundary.
Rc-based parent/back-reference shapes that likely need Weak on the reverse edge.
Release profiles that omit overflow-checks = true in Cargo.toml.
Release profiles that still explicitly use panic = "unwind".
Slice splitting and range indexing on externally-derived offsets without obvious bounds guards.
static mut global state that bypasses the safer shared-state models already in the scanner.
Raw std::thread::spawn blocks that call async work without an explicit runtime handoff.
Unsafe code mixes interior mutability and mutable references in ways that need careful aliasing review.
Unsafe MaybeUninit::assume_init use without proof of full initialization.
Unsafe raw slice construction that depends on lifetime and length invariants.
Unsafe use of get_unchecked without proof of bounds invariants.
Unsafe raw pointer cast that depends on aliasing and lifetime guarantees.
Unsafe Vec::set_len use that requires initialized elements and correct capacity invariants.
Unsafe transmute use that requires layout and validity proof.
